title: Bruschetta al Pomodoro

ingredients.name: thick country bread or sourdough, sliced 1.5 cm

subs.name: thick-sliced baguette or any firm white sandwich bread
subs.notes: avoid soft sliced bread as it will collapse under the topping

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes, deseeded and finely diced

subs.name: roasted capsicum or a mix of tomato and cucumber
subs.notes: roasted capsicum gives a sweeter, smokier bruschetta

subs.name: fresh coriander or mint
subs.notes: coriander gives an Indian twist that works surprisingly well with the tomatoes

subs.name: refined sunflower oil with a pinch of dried oregano
subs.notes: the flavour is milder; the oregano compensates with aroma

steps.text: Dice the tomatoes finely, discard the seeds and watery pulp. Place in a bowl, season with a generous pinch of salt, a drizzle of olive oil, the torn basil and black pepper. Toss and set aside for 10 minutes so the tomatoes release their juices and the flavours meld.

Toast the bread slices on a hot tawa, under a grill or in a dry pan until golden and crisp on both sides — about 2 minutes per side. The bread should have some charred edges and be firm enough to hold the topping without going soggy immediately.
While the toast is still hot, rub each slice firmly with the cut face of a garlic clove. The coarse toasted surface acts like a grater and infuses the bread with raw garlic flavour.
Heap a generous spoonful of the tomato mixture onto each slice. Drizzle lightly with more olive oil and serve within 5 minutes before the bread softens.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bread → thick-sliced baguette or any firm white sandwich bread (avoid soft sliced bread as it will collapse under the topping); olive-oil → refined sunflower oil with a pinch of dried oregano (the flavour is milder; the oregano compensates with aroma); basil → fresh coriander or mint (coriander gives an Indian twist that works surprisingly well with the tomatoes); tomato → roasted capsicum or a mix of tomato and cucumber (roasted capsicum gives a sweeter, smokier bruschetta)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use any thick-sliced bread available (Britannia or Modern sandwich loaves work fine in a pinch, though a baguette from a bakery is better). Toast directly on a flat tawa on medium-high flame — press lightly for even charring. Good olive oil is available at most supermarkets; look for extra-light variety for everyday use. Fresh basil is sold at gourmet stores; coriander makes an equally delicious version with an Indian character.
